title: Berry-phase in a periodically driven single molecule magnet transistor
abstract: We consider the electron transport through a single molecule magnet transistor in the presence of a local transverse magnetic field and ac-driven gate voltage. We calculate the conductance as a function of the electron energy and transverse magnetic field by using the Floquet and Landauer formalism. We show that the time periodic potential causes zero transmission resonances that oscillate as a function of the transverse magnetic field due to the Berry phase interference associated with two quantum tunneling paths. We find that these Berry phase oscillations can be detected in the conductance as a function of the transverse magnetic field for an incoming electron with a specific energy.
summary: This paper shows that an AC-driven gate voltage in a single molecule magnet transistor can be used to detect Berry-phase interference through conductance oscillations. This method is significant because it allows for the observation of these quantum interference effects using unpolarized leads.
main_problem: Investigating how a time-periodic gate voltage and a transverse magnetic field induce Berry-phase interference in the electron transport through a single molecule magnet transistor.
main_result: The study demonstrates that the AC-driven potential creates zero transmission resonances that oscillate with the transverse magnetic field due to Berry-phase interference, and that these oscillations can be detected using unpolarized leads.
method: The authors combine Floquet theory to handle the time-periodic potential with the Landauer formalism to calculate conductance and transmission probability.
model_or_system: A single molecule magnet (SMM) transistor connected to two leads, modeled using a one-dimensional tight-binding chain and the Giant Spin Approximation (GSA) for the SMM spin tunneling.
key_observables: Electron transmission probability, conductance, and zero transmission resonances.
important_parameters: Transverse magnetic field (Bx), gate voltage amplitude (Vg), driving frequency (omega), anisotropy constants (Kz, K), and coupling strength (beta).
main_assumptions: The system is in the strong Coulomb regime where only uncharged or singly charged states are considered, and the exchange coupling is highly anisotropic (J_perp = 0).
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the device setup; Fig 2 displays oscillations of zero transmission resonances; Fig 3 plots transmission probability vs electron energy; Figs 4 and 5 show conductance oscillations as a function of the transverse magnetic field.
paper_structure: The paper begins with the system setup and Hamiltonian definition, moves to the Floquet-Landauer transport formalism, presents analytical and numerical results for transmission and conductance, and concludes with the discussion of Berry-phase detection.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic illustration of a single molecule magnet transistor attached to two leads and controlled by a time dependent gate voltage and a transverse magnetic field.

FIG. 2: Oscillations of the zero transmission resonances as a function of the transverse magnetic field for different values of β. We used the following values Kz = 104J, K = 50J, ω = 3J, ϵOL = 0.5J and J∥= 0.75J for our calculations. Note that it is necessary that the uniaxial anisotropy constant Kz be large in order to have oscillations with large amplitudes.

FIG. 4: The graph shows the oscillations of the conductance as a function of the transverse magnetic field Bx for a SMM with s = 2 due to the Berry phase. We used the following values Vg = 1J, ϵσ = 1J, Kz = 104J, K = 50J, ω = 3J, ϵOL = 0.5J, J∥= 0.75J and β = 0.

FIG. 5: The graph shows the oscillations of the conductance as a function of the transverse magnetic field Bx for a SMM with s = 2 due to the Berry phase. We used the following values Vg = 1J, ϵσ = 1J, Kz = 104J, K = 50J, ω = 3J, ϵOL = 0.5J, J∥= 0.75J and β = −0.5.

FIG. 3: The graph shows the electron transmission in a SMM transistor with homogeneous hopping amplitude for various magnetic field strengths as a function of ϵσ. We used the following values Vg = 1J, Kz = 104J, K = 50J, ω = 3J, ϵOL = 0.5J, J∥= 0.75J and β = 0.
